claim: Lost dogs in survival mode become nocturnal — moving primarily at night when it is quiet with fewer threats — and hiding during the day under porches, in wooded areas, or behind sheds.
stance_target: nocturnal activity shift as behavioral signature of survival mode phase
scope_conditions: dogs that have entered survival mode, typically after first day or more of being lost
quoted_support: Lost dogs often adjust their behavior to move mostly at night when it's quiet and there are fewer threats. During the day, they may hide in wooded areas, under porches, or behind sheds, emerging only after dark to search for food and water.

claim: Even in fear, lost dogs develop predictable routines — revisiting the same safe hiding spots and food sources at consistent times — which is exploitable for trap and camera placement.
stance_target: pattern formation behavior in survival-mode dogs as search intelligence
scope_conditions: dogs that have been lost long enough to establish a territory (typically days to weeks)
quoted_support: Even in fear, dogs crave routine. Once they find a safe hiding spot or food source, they may begin to create a pattern—visiting the same areas around the same time each day. This predictable behavior is valuable for search efforts, as it can help pinpoint where to set up cameras or humane traps.

claim: Dogs in survival mode rarely bark or make noise — they maintain silence to avoid detection by predators or humans — making verbal calling ineffective or counterproductive.
stance_target: silence as behavioral signature of survival mode and reason verbal search is ineffective
quoted_support: Dogs in survival mode rarely bark or make noise. They remain quiet to avoid detection by predators or humans. That's why calling or shouting for your dog can be ineffective—or even counterproductive.

claim: From the lost dog's perspective in survival mode, even its owner appears as 'just another unknown, fast-moving human' and the moment the dog senses pursuit it will run further, often crossing dangerous roads.
stance_target: mechanism by which active searching worsens the dog's displacement
scope_conditions: dogs in survival mode when directly approached by owner
quoted_support: From the dog's perspective, you're no longer 'Mom' or 'Dad'—you're just another unknown, fast-moving human. The moment they sense pursuit, they'll run farther, often crossing dangerous roads or moving into unfamiliar areas.

claim: Stopping active large-group searches is a recommended first action when a dog is in survival mode, because large search parties and name-calling can scatter the dog from its established area.
stance_target: counterproductive nature of large search parties for survival-mode dogs
scope_conditions: dogs confirmed or suspected in survival mode with established sighting patterns
quoted_support: Stop Active Searching: It sounds counterintuitive, but large search parties and calling out names can scatter a dog. Instead, narrow down their location through credible sightings and focus your efforts there.

claim: Survival mode can be triggered not only immediately on escape but also after a subsequent chasing incident — being chased by other people or animals after an initial escape can initiate or deepen the survival state.
stance_target: chasing incidents as a trigger for or escalator of survival mode
scope_conditions: dogs that have been chased post-escape before survival mode was fully established
quoted_support: Chasing incidents: being chased by people or other animals after escaping [listed as a common cause]. Once fear takes over, even small triggers—like voices, vehicles, or human movement—can keep them locked in that state.
